title: Mean and standard deviation of sample means
description: Practice calculating the mean and standard deviation for the sampling distribution of a sample mean.
topic_path: Mean and standard deviation of sample means

assessment_items.question_content:
The birth weights of newborn babies in the United States follow a normal distribution with a mean of $3.4\text{ kg}$ and a standard deviation of $0.6\text{ kg}$. Researchers interested in studying how children gain weight decide to take random samples of $100$ newborn babies and calculate the sample mean birth weight $\bar x$ for each sample.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**

$\mu_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{kg}$

$\sigma_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] $\text{kg}$
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=3.4\text{ kg}$.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=3.4$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

Since there are over $1{,}000$ newborn babies in the population, our sample of $n=100$ newborns is less than $10\%$ of population, and we can assume independence between the newborns in a sample. So the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{0.6}{\sqrt{100}}=\dfrac{0.6}{10}=0.06$

assessment_items.question_content:
A certain region of America with over $500{,}000$ households has a mean household income of $\$70{,}000$ with a standard deviation of $\$20{,}000$. Economists plan to take random samples of $625$ households and calculate the sample mean income $\bar x$ for each sample.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**  

$\mu_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars

$\sigma_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] dollars
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=70{,}000$ dollars.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=70{,}000$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

Since there are over $500{,}000$ households in the population, our sample of $n=625$ households is less than $10\%$ of population, and we can assume independence between the households in a sample. So the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{20{,}000}{\sqrt{625}}=\dfrac{20{,}000}{25}=800$

###Answer:

$\mu_{\bar x}=70000$ dollars

$\sigma_{\bar x}=800$ dollars

assessment_items.question_content:
A car manufacturer produced $5{,}000$ cars for a limited edition model. Dealers sold all of these cars at mean price of $\$36{,}000$ with a standard deviation of $\$3{,}000$. Suppose we were to take random samples of $9$ of these cars and calculate the sample mean price $\bar x$ for each sample.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**  

$\mu_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars

$\sigma_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] dollars
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=36{,}000$ dollars.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=36{,}000$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

Since there are over $5{,}000$ cars in the population, our sample of $n=9$ cars is less than $10\%$ of population, and we can assume independence between the cars in a sample. So the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$  can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{3{,}000}{\sqrt{9}}=\dfrac{3{,}000}{3}=1{,}000$

###Answer:

$\mu_{\bar x}=36{,}000$ dollars

$\sigma_{\bar x}=1{,}000$ dollars

assessment_items.question_content:
Every year, more than $100{,}000$ testers take the Law School Admission Test (LSAT). One year, the scores had a mean and standard deviation of approximately $151$ and $9$ points, respectively. Suppose that in the scoring process, test officials audit random samples of $36$ tests, which involves calculating the sample mean score $\bar x$ for each sample.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**  

$\mu_{\bar x}\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] points

$\sigma_{\bar x}\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] points
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu\approx151$ points.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu\approx151$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

Since there are more than $100{,}000$ testers in the population, a sample of $n=36$ testers is less than $10\%$ of population, and we can assume independence between the testers in a sample. So the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\approx\dfrac{9}{\sqrt{36}}\approx\dfrac{9}{6}\approx1.5$

###Answer:

$\mu_{\bar x}\approx151$ points

$\sigma_{\bar x}\approx1.5$ points

assessment_items.question_content:
An apple producer sells their product in bags that each contain $16$ apples. These apples have a mean weight of $80$ grams and a standard deviation of $5$ grams. Suppose that each bag represents an [[☃ definition 1]] of apples, and we calculate the sample mean weight $\bar x$ of the apples in each bag.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**  

$\mu_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] grams

$\sigma_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] grams
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=80$ grams.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=80$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

It's safe to assume that there are more than $160$ apples in the population, so a sample of $n=16$ apples is less than $10\%$ of population, and we can assume independence between the apples in a sample. So the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{5}{\sqrt{16}}=\dfrac{5}{4}=1.25$

###Answer:

$\mu_{\bar x}=80$ grams

$\sigma_{\bar x}=1.25$ grams

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A light bulb manufacturer claims that a certain type of bulb they make has a mean lifetime of $1000$ hours and a standard deviation of $20$ hours. Each package sold contains $4$ of these bulbs. Suppose that each package represents an [[☃ definition 1]] of bulbs, and we calculate the sample mean lifetime $\bar x$ of the bulbs in each package.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**  

$\mu_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] hours

$\sigma_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] hours
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=1000$ hours.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=1000$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

It's safe to assume that there are more than $40$ of these bulbs in the population, so a sample of $n=4$ bulbs is less than $10\%$ of population, and we can assume independence between the bulbs in a sample. So the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{20}{\sqrt{4}}=\dfrac{20}{2}=10$

###Answer:

$\mu_{\bar x}=1000$ hours

$\sigma_{\bar x}=10$ hours

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lucille has a collection of more than $500$ songs on her phone that have a mean duration of $215$ seconds and a standard deviation of $35$ seconds. Suppose that every week she makes a playlist by taking an [[☃ definition 1]] of $49$ of these songs, and we calculate the sample mean duration $\bar x$ of the songs in each sample.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**  

$\mu_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] seconds

$\sigma_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] seconds
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=215$ seconds.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=215$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

Since there are more than $500$ songs in the population, a sample of $n=49$ songs is less than $10\%$ of population, and we can assume independence between the songs in a sample. So the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{35}{\sqrt{49}}=\dfrac{35}{7}=5$

###Answer:

$\mu_{\bar x}=215$ seconds

$\sigma_{\bar x}=5$ seconds

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Suppose that among the population of smartphone owners with $16\text{GB}$ of storage, the mean amount of storage used is $13\text{GB}$ and the standard deviation is $3\text{GB}$. A consumer research group regularly takes random samples of $50$ of these customers and calculates the mean amount of storage $\bar x$ used in each sample.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**  
*You may round to one decimal place.*

$\mu_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] gigabytes

$\sigma_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] gigabytes
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=13$ gigabytes.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=13$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

It's safe to assume that there are more than $500$ customers in the population, so a sample of $n=50$ customers is less than $10\%$ of population, and we can assume independence between the customers in a sample. So the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{50}}\approx\dfrac{3}{7.071}\approx0.424$

###Answer:

$\mu_{\bar x}=13$ gigabytes

$\sigma_{\bar x}\approx0.4$ gigabytes

assessment_items.question_content:
A machine automatically dispenses a beverage of a desired size. When set to "small," the machine dispenses varying amounts of liquid with a mean of $275\,\text{mL}$ and standard deviation of $10\,\text{mL}$. Suppose that we take random samples of $5$ of these drinks and calculate the mean amount of liquid $\bar x$ in each sample. We can assume that individual drinks are independent.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**  
*You may round to one decimal place.*

$\mu_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{mL}$

$\sigma_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] $\text{mL}$
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=275\,\text{mL}$.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=275$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

We were told to assume independence between the $n=5$ drinks in a sample, so the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{5}}\approx\dfrac{10}{2.236}\approx4.472$

assessment_items.question_content:
Karina plays a video game that is scored by round. In about $500$ rounds, her career average is $20$ points per round with a standard deviation of $5$ points per round. Suppose we take random samples of $3$ past rounds and calculate the mean number of points $\bar x$ that she scored in each sample.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**  
*You may round to one decimal place.*

$\mu_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] points

$\sigma_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] points
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=20$ points.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=20$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

Since there are more than $500$ rounds in the population, a sample of $n=3$ rounds is less than $10\%$ of population, and we can assume independence between the rounds in a sample. So the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{5}{\sqrt{3}}\approx\dfrac{5}{1.732}\approx2.887$

assessment_items.question_content:
A small school with $60$ total students records how many of their students attend school on each of the $180$ days in a school year. The mean number of students in attendance daily is $55$ students and the standard deviation is $4$ students. Suppose that we take random samples of $5$ school days and calculate the mean number of students $\bar x$ in attendance on those days in each sample.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**  
*You may round to one decimal place.*

$\mu_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] students

$\sigma_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] students
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=55$ students.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=55$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

Since there are $180$ days in the population, a sample of $n=5$ days is less than $10\%$ of population, and we can assume independence between the days in a sample. So the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{4}{\sqrt{5}}\approx\dfrac{4}{2.236}\approx1.789$

assessment_items.question_content:
Shoppers at a mall have a mean weight of $70\,\text{kg}$ with a standard deviation of $10\,\text{kg}$. An elevator at the mall holds a maximum of $6$ people, and safety engineers are curious about the average weight of shoppers on a full elevator. Suppose that we take random samples of $6$ shoppers and calculate the mean weight $\bar x$ of the shoppers in each sample.

**Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$.**  
*You may round to one decimal place.*

$\mu_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{kg}$

$\sigma_{\bar x}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] $\text{kg}$
hints:
###The mean of the sampling distribution:

The mean of the sampling distribution of a sample mean $\bar x$ is equal to the population mean:

$\mu_{\bar{x}}=\mu$

The population mean is reported as $\mu=70\,\text{kg}$.

So $\mu_{\bar x}=\mu=70$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

It's safe to assume that there are more than $60$ shoppers in the population, so a sample of $n=6$ shoppers is less than $10\%$ of population, and we can assume independence between the shoppers in a sample. So the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar x$ can be found using this formula:

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is

$\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}=\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{6}}\approx\dfrac{10}{2.449}\approx4.083$
